title: Sourdough discard banana bread
subtitle: A tangy quick loaf that puts leftover starter to use
excerpt: Sourdough discard adds a mild background tang to banana bread without changing the method. The ripe bananas provide sweetness and moisture, and the discard loosens the batter and deepens the flavour. No yeast, no proofing time.
sourceNotes: Using sourdough discard in quick breads is a modern home-baking practice that became common in the 2010s alongside the growth of home sourdough culture. The banana bread base follows the established British quick-bread method. No single public-domain text covers this exact combination; the recipe is synthesised from both traditions.

recipe.freezeNotes: Slice the cooled loaf and freeze flat in a sealed bag for up to 3 months. Toast from frozen or thaw at room temperature.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The loaf is best on day two after the flavours settle. Wrap well at room temperature.

glossaryTerms.definition: Sourdough discard is the portion of starter removed before a feeding to keep its volume manageable. It is not active enough to leaven bread on its own, but it contains lactic and acetic acids that add flavour. In quick breads it acts as a flavouring and partial liquid, not as a leavener. The leavening here comes from baking powder and bicarbonate of soda.

content.text: as part of the wet mix. It adds a gentle tang and loosens the batter without changing how the loaf is made. The bananas must be very ripe, the skin more black than yellow; underripe fruit is too starchy and too mild.
The discard can be straight from the fridge. Cold or room-temperature, it works the same way here. Use any hydration, but thin (100%) discard blends in most easily.
Heat the oven to 170 degrees C. Grease a 900 g loaf tin and line with baking paper. Mash the bananas thoroughly in a large bowl until no large chunks remain.

Stir the 100 g sourdough discard, 80 g melted butter, 120 g light brown sugar, 2 eggs and 1 tsp vanilla extract into the mashed banana until combined. Sift the 200 g flour, 1 tsp baking powder, half a teaspoon bicarbonate of soda and half a teaspoon salt over the wet mixture and fold together until no dry flour remains. Do not overmix.

Pour the batter into the tin and bake for 55 to 60 minutes until a skewer inserted in the centre comes out with just a few moist crumbs and the top is a deep brown with a centre crack. The loaf will feel firm when gently pressed. Cool in the tin for 10 minutes, then turn out onto a rack and leave for at least 20 minutes before slicing.

items.problem: Loaf tastes flat with no tang
items.solution: The discard may have been very fresh or refrigerated for only a short time. Older discard (kept in the fridge for 5 to 7 days since the last feeding) has more acidity. Use older discard on the next bake.
The bananas were not ripe enough or the discard was thick. Add a splash of whole milk (about 2 tablespoons) to loosen the batter before baking.
Banana breads run dense and moist. Test at 55 minutes; the skewer should come out with a few crumbs but not wet batter. Cover loosely with foil if the top is already dark and give it 5 more minutes.
